coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot/Co-MOF nanocoral mediated fluorescence-scattering ratiometric sensor for highly sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase
coredata.dc:creator: Yao, Luxuan
He, Shan
Chen, Yiyu
Lian, Huiting
Liu, Bin
Lai, Chengcai
Wei, Xiaofeng
coredata.dc:description: Abnormal expression of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) in serum has received considerable attention in health monitoring and disease diagnosis. However, conventional optical analysis based on a single signal must compromise background interference and limited sensitivity in trace analysis. As an alternative candidate, the ratiometric approach depends on the self-calibration of two independent signals in a single test to minimize interferences from the background for accurate identification. Here, a carbon dot/cobalt-metal organic framework nanocoral (CD/Co-MOF NC) mediated fluorescence-scattering ratiometric sensor has been developed for simple, stable, and highly sensitive detection of ALP. ALP-responsive phosphate production was used to coordinate cobalt ion and collapse the CD/Co-MOF NC, resulting in the recovery of fluorescence signal from dissociative CDs and the decrease of second-order scattering (SOS) signal from the cracked CD/Co-MOF NC. The ligand-substituted reaction and the optical ratiometric signal transduction provide a rapid and reliable chemical sensing mechanism. The ratiometric sensor effectively converted ALP into a ratio signal of fluorescence-scattering dual emission throughout a wide linear concentration range of six orders of magnitude with a detection limit of 0.6 mU/L. In addition, self-calibration of fluorescence-scattering ratiometric method can reduce background interference and improve sensitivity in serum, approaching recoveries of ALP from 98.4% to 101.8%. Due to the above advantages, the CD/Co-MOF NC mediated fluorescence-scattering ratiometric sensor readily provides rapid and stable quantitative detection of ALP as a promising in vitro analytical method for clinical diagnostics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Metal organic framework
Second-order scattering
Fluorescence
Alkaline phosphatase

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Working principle of CD/Co-MOF NC mediated fluorescence-scattering ratiometric sensor
Condition optimization of the ratiometric sensor
Selectivity of the ratiometric sensor and serum sample analysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge the financial support provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 21904043 , 82204751 , 21575044 ), and the Foundation of Graphene Powder & Composite Research Center of Fujian Province ( 2017H2001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Material characterization: (A, B) SEM images of CD/Co-MOF NC and (C) TEM images of CD. (D) XRD images of CD, Co-MOF nanoflower, and CD/Co-MOF NC. (E) FT-IR absorption spectrum of CD. (F) UV–vis spectrum, fluorescence excitation spectrum, and fluorescence emission spectrum of CD.

(A) Feasibility validation based on the fluorescence and SOS spectra at different conditions: (a) Only CD, (b) Only CD/Co-MOF NC, (c) CD/Co-MOF NC + AAP, (d) CD/Co-MOF NC + PO4 3−, (e) CD/Co-MOF NC + AAP + ALP. (B) Histogram of the peak intensities of F436 and S739 vs different PO4 3− concentration and plot of the ratio of F436/S739 vs different PO4 3− concentration. The standard deviation was obtained from three parallel measurements.
Optimization of the concentrations of (A) Co2+, (B) 2-ICA, and (C) AAP. The error bars represent standard deviations from three parallel measurements.

Ratiometrically quantitative detection: (A) Fluorescence and SOS spectra of the CD/Co-MOF NC mediated ratiometric sensor in different concentrations of ALP. (B) Calibration curve of ΔR% vs different concentrations of ALP. The standard deviation was obtained from three parallel measurements.

Selectivity investigation of the CD/Co-MOF NC mediated ratiometric sensor for different enzymes by their fluorescence and SOS spectra (A) and histogram of Δratio of F436/S739 (B). The error bars represent standard deviations from three parallel measurements.
Schematic diagram of CD/Co-MOF NC mediated fluorescence-scattering ratiometric sensor.
Comparison of various methods for ALP detection.
Detection of ALP spiked in human serum.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: aRelative standard deviations were obtained from three parallel measurements.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot/Co-MOF nanocoral mediated fluorescence-scattering ratiometric sensor for highly sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Abnormal expression of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) in serum has received considerable attention in health monitoring and disease diagnosis. However, conventional optical analysis based on a single signal must compromise background interference and limited sensitivity in trace analysis. As an alternative candidate, the ratiometric approach depends on the self-calibration of two independent signals in a single test to minimize interferences from the background for accurate identification. Here, a carbon dot/cobalt-metal organic framework nanocoral (CD/Co-MOF NC) mediated fluorescence-scattering ratiometric sensor has been developed for simple, stable, and highly sensitive detection of ALP. ALP-responsive phosphate production was used to coordinate cobalt ion and collapse the CD/Co-MOF NC, resulting in the recovery of fluorescence signal from dissociative CDs and the decrease of second-order scattering (SOS) signal from the cracked CD/Co-MOF NC. The ligand-substituted reaction and the optical ratiometric signal transduction provide a rapid and reliable chemical sensing mechanism. The ratiometric sensor effectively converted ALP into a ratio signal of fluorescence-scattering dual emission throughout a wide linear concentration range of six orders of magnitude with a detection limit of 0.6 mU/L. In addition, self-calibration of fluorescence-scattering ratiometric method can reduce background interference and improve sensitivity in serum, approaching recoveries of ALP from 98.4% to 101.8%. Due to the above advantages, the CD/Co-MOF NC mediated fluorescence-scattering ratiometric sensor readily provides rapid and stable quantitative detection of ALP as a promising in vitro analytical method for clinical diagnostics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP), as a typical isozyme, is extensively involved in the dephosphorylation of small molecules, nucleic acids and proteins in life activities [1]. Abnormal expression of ALP in serum is generally associated with main liver and bone diseases, including biliary obstruction, osteochondrosis, leukoaraiosis, metabolic disorders, leukemia, aplastic anemia, etc. Standardized analysis of ALP levels has been widely utilized as a reference biomarker in health monitoring and disease diagnosis. Therefore, the development of simple, efficient, and sensitive ALP detection methods is urgently needed in clinical medicine. To date, numerous chemo-sensors have been proposed for quantifying ALP by colorimetry [2,3].fluorescence [4]. [5],chemiluminescence [6], electrochemistry [7,8], and so forth. For example, Liu et al. [9] proposed a novel nanozyme-based colorimetric chemosensor for ALP detection, where the first synthesized FeCo nanoparticles incorporated porous zeolitic imidazolate framework nanocages showed enhanced oxidase-like activity with excellent stability and reproducibility. Similarly, some enzyme-mimic nanomaterials, including CQD-silver nanocomposites [10], manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheets [11], have been applied as ALP-responsive colorimetric catalyst to replace expensive and environmentally sensitive natural enzyme. However, restricted sensitivity and unstable backgrounds by visual reading still limit the development of colorimetric chemosensing of ALP. Besides, fluorescence chemosensors have also been developed for ALP detection with low detection limits and broad detection ranges. Zhao et al. [12] constructed a Cu2+-thiamine redox system based fluorometric method for sensitive quantification of ALP. Facile reaction condition and homogeneous system both supported a sensitively fluorescent readout of ALP in serum. Additionally, energy resonance transfer [13], aggregation-induced fluorescence [14], and other fluorometric methods have been reported for high-sensitive detection of ALP. Facing different real samples, especially in complex clinical biomatrix, specific fluorescent peak usually is obtained with random background interference, which hinders in vitro detection of trace biomarkers. Therefore, a simple, background-resistant, and sensitive detection method should be constructed for the rapid quantitative detection of ALP.

Many common optically analytical methods by single signal have to compromise to background interference and limited sensitivity in trace analysis [15]. Various sources of interference in clinical sample includes reaction temperature, pH, probe concentration, excitation intensity, salt concentration, small molecule, and protein. The subtraction of background signal of blank samples in parallel can reduce the background interference to a certain extent, however the tedious operation is generally repelled as a rapid detection. As an alternative candidate, the ratiometric analysis depends on self-calibration of two independent signals in one assay to diminish the background interferences for accurate identification [16]. Ratiometric methods include the ratiometric optical method [17], ratiometric electrical method [18],ratiometric photoelectric [19] method, were applied for ratiometric fluorescence sensing. The material-mediated ratiometric fluorescence method [20] effectively weakened the background interference via a simple ratio treatment between two emissions. However, the complicated synthesis and unstable conjugation of composites are a challenge of analytical cost and stability in practical application. For instance, organic polymer materials [21] require a long synthetic time, and precious metal nanoparticles [22] are costly and non-biodegradable. Taken together, an inexpensive, easy to be synthetic, non-toxic, fluorescence-controllable material becomes a critical factor for ratiometric fluorescence analysis.

Carbon dots (CDs) are broadly used in the field of detection due to good optical properties, non-toxicity, low cost, and chemical inertness. Compared with common precious metal nanoparticles or organic fluorophores, CDs have better fluorescence quantum yield, environmental compatibility, and simple synthetic process [23]. The applications of CDs have been proposed in various fields, such as drug loading in medicine [24], anti-counterfeit markings in life [25]. For instance, Zhao et al. [26] prepared three different colors of carbon dots by a one-step hydrothermal method. The prepared carbon dots can be effectively used for information encryption and advanced anti-counterfeiting. Lin et al. [27] fabricated a series of novel curcumin-derived carbon dots (Cur-CQDs) by a simple and controllable dry-heating treatment. The distinctively pyrolytic curcumin polymers on the CQDs contribute to excellent antiviral properties, water solubility, and biocompatibility, effectively inhibiting the infection of EV71 virus in various stages. Extensive synthesis methods and surface functionalization provide flexible editability and versatility for carbon dot applications.

Inspired by the aforementioned research progress, we developed a CD/cobalt-metal organic framework nanocoral (CD/Co-MOF NC) mediated fluorescence-scattering ratiometric sensor for simple, stable, and highly sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase. ALP-responsive phosphate increase was introduced to coordinate cobalt ion and collapse the CD/Co-MOF NC, leading to the recovery of fluorescence signal from dissociative CDs and the reduction of second-order scattering (SOS) signal from the CD/Co-MOF NC. In the system, ligand substitution reactions and optical ratiometric signals ensure a fast and robust chemical sensing mechanism. Self-calibration of fluorescence-scattering ratiometric treatment can reduce background interference and improve the sensitivity in complex biomatrixes. As the traditional ratiometric fluorescence sensors, tremendous fluorescent probes were constructed with multiple fluorescent reporters for fluorescent dual-emission, fluorescence resonance energy transfer, and distance-based fluorescence quenching. However, cross interference of emissions, cumbersome synthetics of probes, and environment unfriendly post-use disposal still limit the development of ratiometric fluorescence methods. In contrast, our proposed fluorescence-scattering ratiometric sensor can eliminate cross interference of fluorescent emissions. CDs in the ratiometric probe can be sustainably obtained from green and cheap sources, and simply formed into the CD/Co-MOF NC. With those significant features, CD/Co-MOF NC mediated fluorescence-scattering ratiometric sensor can readily achieve rapid and stable quantitative detection of ALP as a potential in vitro analytic approach in clinical diagnostics.

Cobalt nitrate, dimethylimidazole (2-ICA), o-phenylenediamine, glucose oxidase (GOx), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), pepsin were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Citric acid, L-ascorbic acid (AA), 2-phosphate sesquimagnesium salt hydrate (AAP), lactate oxidase (LOx), urate oxidase (UOx) were obtained from Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and alkaline phosphatase were used from Thermo Fisher. Serum samples from healthy individuals were provided by the Affiliated Hospital of Putian University (Record Number PTUAH-2021–021-B). All other reagents were analytical grade, and all buffer solutions were prepared with ultrapure Milli-Q water (18.2 MΩ cm, Millipore Inc., USA).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained by a JEM-2100F HRTEM at 200 kV (Thermo Fisher Scientific), and field emission electron microscopy (SEM) images were recorded via a JSM-7610F (PlusJapan corporation). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were determined using a Rigaku D/MAX-2550 X-ray diffractometer (Nippon Neo-Confucianism Corporation). An FTIR-8400S was used to obtain the Fourier transform infrared spectrum. UV–vis spectra were collected by a Shimadzu UV2600. The SOS spectra and fluorescence spectra were recorded by an F7000 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Tokyo).

The CDs were synthesized with reference to a classical method [28]. First, 0.21 g of o-phenylenediamine and 0.50 g of citric acid monohydrate were mixed in 10 mL of water. After dissolution, the mixture solution was transferred to an autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 5 h. Then, the precipitate was separated from the mixture solution using a centrifuge (10000 rpm, 5 min), and the yellow supernatant was filtered through a 400 μm filter membrane. After filtration, the mixture was placed in a 1000 Da dialysis bag to remove residual impurities. Finally, CD powder was obtained after drying in a refrigerated vacuum dryer at −44 °C, 23 Pa for 8 h, and 10 mg/mL CDs were prepared in water as the stock solution before use.

20 μL of cobalt nitrate (0.50 M) was added into 2.0 mL of CDs stock solution with a quick stirring for 5 min. After complete dissolution, 60 μL of 2-ICA (0.50 M) was dropped by gentle stirring for 30 s to obtain the original CD/Co-MOF NC as the reporter. Besides, different concentrations of cobalt nitrate, 2-ICA were optimized in the same synthetic process of CD/Co-MOF NC.

Different concentrations of ALP solution as targets were prepared from 0.001–100 U/L. The final concentration of AAP was 0.2 mM in 10 mM Tris-HCl buffer, containing 2 mM NaCl (pH 7.4). The enzymatic reaction was incubated at 37 °C for 40 min with the final volume of 50 μL. After the full reaction, the samples were mixed with the original CD/Co-MOF NC (1.95 mL) with gentle shaking at room temperature for 30 s. The final fluorescence/SOS dual-signal of samples were measured by the spectrofluorometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working principle of CD/Co-MOF NC mediated fluorescence-scattering ratiometric sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The design and working principle of the CD/Co-MOF NC mediated fluorescence-scattering ratiometric sensor is schematically illustrated in Scheme 1 . Before ALP introduction, the PO4 3--responsive ratiometric probe is constructed by the composition of CD/Co-MOF NC. All synthetic processes are stated in the Experimental Section. The SEM images in Fig. 1 A and B shows the morphology of the composite CD/Co-MOF NC with a stacked nanocoral structure. The original spectrum of CD/Co-MOF NC (left illustration in Scheme 1) is recorded as the control with a weak fluorescence signal at 436 nm and a strong SOS signal at 739 nm, indicating a low ratiometric signal of F436/S739. In the presence of ALP, the substrate AAP is digested enzymatically to generate AA and PO4 3−. The free PO4 3− small molecules enter the pore structure of CD/Co-MOF NC quickly and unhindered, and coordinated with cobalt ions to form more stable Co3(PO4)2. With the loss of cobalt cores, the CD/Co-MOF NC gradually collapses and releases the diffusible CDs back into the homogeneous solution. Finally, as shown in right illustration in Scheme 1, the fluorescence signal recovers from the unconstrained CDs, meanwhile, the SOS signal decreases due to the cleavage of large CD/Co-MOF NCs, resulting in a significant increase of F436/S739. Therefore, the higher the ALP concentration, the stronger the fluorescence signal, and the weaker the SOS signal, leading to a larger ratiometric signal. The positive correlation of the ratiometric signal of F436/S739 with the ALP concentration forms the foundation of the fluorescence-scattering ratiometrically quantitative detection.

To validate the effective production of the ratiometric sensor, CD/Co-MOF NC was characterized and compared with related precursor and homologous MOF. First, the morphological characteristics of CD/Co-MOF NC and Co-MOF nanoflower were examined by SEM. As shown in Fig. 1A and B, the CD/Co-MOF NC constructed a polyporous layered nanocoral with a wide surface area, allowing small molecules to easily penetrating. Due to electrostatic gravitation [15], Co2+ prioritized to adsorb on the negatively charged CD surface, then coordinated with 2-ICA and assembled into CD/Co-MOF NC. As a contrast, the coordination of dispersive Co2+ and 2-ICA was carried out without CD, resulting in the formation of a nanoflower structure in Fig. S1. The result of morphological difference between CD/Co-MOF NC and Co-MOF nanoflower preliminarily confirmed a successful hybridization of CD and Co-MOF. The key precursor, CD, showed a concentrated particulate diameter around 3 nm–4 nm by TEM as depicted in Fig. 1C. Additionally, XRD patterns of CD, CD/Co-MOF NC and Co-MOF nanoflower were measured in Fig. 1D to verify the actual composition. A broad diffraction peak was measured at 2θ 20.0° ascribed to CD, while disappeared in the XRD pattern of CD/Co-MOF NC, which indicating an effective encapsulation of CD leading to the lattice distortion in CD/Co-MOF NC. For the pattern of Co-MOF nanoflower, several dispersed weak peaks appeared at 2θ in the range of 10.0°–30.0° due to diverse crystal orientation. However, the pattern of CD/Co-MOF NC revealed a spiculate and robust diffraction peak at 2θ 28.5°, which was attributable to the layered structure of nanocoral. The XRD results further validated a successful fabrication of CD/Co-MOF NC. Based on UV–vis and FT-IR spectra as displayed in Fig. 1E and F, the absorption peak located at 367 nm was contributed to the n-π* transition of CN and CO by the specific peaks of 1760 cm−1, 1630 cm−1, 1570 cm−1. Besides, the optimal excitation wavelength (Ex) and emission wavelength (Em) of CD respectively were tested at 365 nm and 436 nm.

A series of experiments were designed and carried out to demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed CD/Co-MOF NC mediated fluorescence-scattering ratiometric sensing method. Parallel controlled experiments included different materials, reagents and target were tested for fluorescence and SOS dual signal output. As shown in Fig. 2 A, line a in the presence of only CD shows a strong F436 signal and a negligible S739 signal. Because the unconstrained CD owned stable fluorescence emission, nanosized CD spheroidal particle merely triggered a slight scattering. Before the inspection of CD/Co-MOF NC, its precursors, Co2+ and 2-ICA, were mixed with CD to observe the change of F436 signal and S739 signal. As illustrated in Figs. S2 and 21.6% decrease of F436 by Co2+, 13.7% increase of F436 by 2-ICA, and negligible change of S739 by them were measured, where the weak influence of precursors for F436 signal had been eliminated by condition optimization. In the presence of only CD/Co-MOF NC (line b), the F436 signal decreased to one-fifth of the original with an enhanced S739 signal up to 10 times, due to the encapsulation of CD in CD/Co-MOF NC with the increase in composite size. When PO4 3− was introduced (line d), recovered F436 signal and decreased S739 signal were simultaneously measured, which proved that the PO4 3--responsive cleavage of CD/Co-MOF NC is effective. Accord with our proposed principle, parts of CD were released with recovered fluorescence and CD/Co-MOF NC reduced with lower SOS. Conversely, the introduction of AAP (line c) didn't change the intensity of two spectral peaks, implying a stable background signal of enzyme-catalyzed substrate. Once we activated the enzymatic hydrolysis of AAP by ALP (line e), a significant increase at F436 and decrease at S739 were obtained with the mean intensity equivalent to that by PO4 3−. Taken together, our results clearly demonstrated the feasibility of our ratiometric sensing principle to achieve a fluorescence-scattering dual-signal response for quantitative detection of ALP.

Besides, the relation of ratio of F436/S739 and PO4 3− concentration were detailly investigated to confirm the capability of PO4 3− to collapse the CD/Co-MOF NC. As shown in Fig. 2B, the ratio of F436/S739 increased with the increase of PO4 3− concentration from 0.001 μM to 10 μM, and then sharply decreased in the presence of 100 μM PO4 3− due to the aggregation of Co3(PO4)2. The result verified a positive correlation of the ratio of F436/S739 and the PO4 3− concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Condition optimization of the ratiometric sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Several critical parameters were optimized for stable background signal and low F436/S739 ratio without ALP, namely, the concentrations of Co2+, 2-ICA and AAP. In the synthesis of CD/Co-MOF NC, excess Co2+ inhibited the F436 signal of free CDs. To minimize the inhibition of redundant Co2+ while the F436 recovering by ALP, different concentrations of Co2+ were investigated for a saturated level of fluorescence quenching at a certain concentration of CDs. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the F436 signal decreased with the increase of the Co2+ concentration from 1.25 mM to 5 mM, reaching a platform after 5 mM. So, 5 mM was selected as the optimal concentration of Co2+. Besides, the concentration of 2-ICA was optimized for the maximization of F436/S739 ratio, achieving a complete encapsulation of CD. As displayed in Fig. 3B, a pair of opposite tendencies from F436 signal and S739 signal were detected as the increase of the 2-ICA concentration, inducing a sharp decrease of F436/S739 ratio from 2.5 mM to 15 mM. Considering the minimization of F436/S739 ratio without target as control level, 15 mM was chosen as the optimal concentration of 2-ICA. In the ALP-based enzymic degradation, the concentration of AAP as the substrate to supply the PO4 3− is significant to affect the recovery of F436/S739 ratio. Fig. 3C shows a steady F436 signal was recorded when the concentration of AAP was less than 0.2 mM. However, a non-target responsive increase of F436 signal was measured in the concentration range from 0.25 mM to 10 mM, which should be carefully regulated to avoid the false positive. Hence, we selected 0.2 mM as the optimal concentration of AAP.

After the optimization, the proposed CD/Co-MOF NC mediated fluorescence-scattering ratiometric sensor was applied to quantitative detection of ALP. As illustrated in Fig. 4 A, the F436 signal increased while the S739 signal decreased along with the increase of the concentration of ALP in the range from 0 U/L to 100 U/L. Taking the initial F436/S739 ratio (R0) without ALP as the blank, the F436/S739 ratios (R) of different concentrations of ALP were calculated for ΔR% and plotted versus the concentrations. Herein, the result of ΔR% was obtained by the formula, ΔR%=(R–R0)/R0 × 100%. Fig. 4B exhibits an excellent linear relationship between the ΔR% and the ALP concentration in the range of 1 mU/L to 100 U/L with the R
2 value of 0.934. The regression equation was ΔR% = 0.2349 log
10 C + 0.7562 with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.6 mU/L based on 3σ/slope, where 3σ means 3-folds of the standard deviation vs the blank signal. Using single-signal F436 (See Fig. S3), ΔF% = 0.1588 log
10 C + 0.4721 was fitted as the calibration curve between the ΔF% and the ALP concentration. To compare two means of signal transduction, the slope of the calibration curve of dual emission signal-based method is nearly 1.5 times that of the single signal-based method. Compared with nanocomposite-based optical sensors in Table 1 , our CD/Co-MOF NC mediated ratiometric sensor has higher detection sensitivity than single signal methods. The LOD of our work approaches 1–3 orders of magnitude lower than those of fluorescent methods, as well as 2–4 orders of magnitude lower than colorimetric methods. Moreover, five orders of magnitude linear dynamic range by the ratiometric sensor was far broader than those of other single-signal optical methods. Consequently, these results confirmed that highly sensitive detection performance of the CD/Co-MOF NC mediated ratiometric sensor for rapid quantification of ALP and established a stable foundation for us to investigate its capacity in the real sample analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the ratiometric sensor and serum sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the application of the ratiometric sensor in complex clinical matrixes, the selectivity of CD/Co-MOF NC-based sensor was evaluated for the distinguishment of ALP and other enzymes in serum in Fig. 5 . Several common enzymes, including GOx, pepsin, LOx, HRP, UOx were detected with 10-fold concentration of ALP. As presented, only the specific target, ALP (10 U/L) showed the Δratio of F436/S739 accessing 0.55, whereas Δratios less than 0.15 from other enzymes. Here, the Δratio of F436/S739 was calculated by subtracting the F436/S739 ratio of blank group from the F436/S739 ratio of enzyme group. This result indicated the high selectivity of our ratiometric sensor.

To validate the CD/Co-MOF NC-based ratiometric sensing method in practical applications, serum samples were spiked with ALP were measured using our sensor. As listed in Table 2 , all the recovery values from different concentrations of ALP were determined at the desirable level among 98.4%–101.8% with all the relative standard deviations (RSDs) between 1.4% and 13% in human serum (100-fold diluted in Tris-HCl buffer). Taken together, the proposed CD/Co-MOF NC mediated fluorescence-scattering ratiometric sensor can be effectively utilized for the quantitative detection of ALP in complex serum samples.

In summary, we have developed a CD/Co-MOF NC mediated fluorescence-scattering ratiometric sensor for simple, stable and highly sensitive detection of ALP. The sustainable carbon dots from green source were easily assembled for CD/Co-MOF NC. The ALP-responsive configurational change of composites effectively converts ALP concentrations into fluorescent recovery from free CD and SOS reduction from the disintegration of CD/Co-MOF NC. In comparison with single-signal detection, dual emission signal-based ratiometric readout achieves self-calibration to minimize interferences with a high signal-to-background ratio, near 1.5-fold slope value as that by single fluorescent recovery of CD. The ratiometric sensor exhibits a wide detection range of five orders of magnitude and high sensitivity with a low detection limit of 0.6 mU/L. Rapid quantitative detection of ALP demonstrated the reliable selectivity of CD/Co-MOF NC-based ratiometric sensor, meanwhile verified the desired robustness with recoveries of ALP from 98.4% to 101.8% in complex serum matrix. The CD/Co-MOF NC mediated fluorescence-scattering ratiometric sensor shows great potential for the practical application of ALP detection in biochemical analysis and disease diagnostics.

Luxuan Yao: Conceptualization, methodology, formal analysis, writing, review, and editing. Shan He: Methodology, writing, review, and editing. Yiyu Chen: Methodology, writing, review, and editing. Huiting Lian: Methodology and supervision of experiments. Bin Liu: Funding acquisition, and supervision of experiments. Xiaofeng Wei: Methodology, writing, review, editing, and funding acquisition. Chengcai Lai: Project administration, review, editing, funding acquisition.

Luxuan Yao: Conceptualization, methodology, formal analysis, writing, review, and editing. Shan He: Methodology, formal analysis, editing. Yiyu Chen: Writing, review, editing. Huiting Lian: Methodology and supervision of experiments. Bin Liu: Review and supervision of experiments. Chengcai Lai: Project administration, review and funding acquisition. Xiaofeng Wei: Methodology, writing, review, editing, and funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial support provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 21904043, 82204751, 21575044), and the Foundation of Graphene Powder & Composite Research Center of Fujian Province (2017H2001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2023.124863.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Assays for alkaline phosphatase activity: progress and prospects
TrAC-Trend. Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Tang, H. Chen, H. He, C. Ma, Assays for alkaline phosphatase activity: Progress and prospects, TrAC-Trend. Anal. Chem. 113 (2019) 32-43.
